title: More Tariff Pass-Through Is in the Pipeline - Liberty Street Economics
gist: NY Fed regional business surveys (May 2026) find that nearly half of firms that directly paid tariffs still plan further price increases to recoup costs, some more than six months out, implying tariff-driven inflation is a gradual, multi-quarter process rather than a one-time shock.

insights.claim: Nearly half of firms that have paid tariffs still plan additional price increases to offset these costs, with some expecting to raise prices six months or more into the future.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Signals more CPI/PPI upward pressure from tariffs is still in the pipeline more than a year after tariffs began, arguing against assuming the price-level adjustment is complete.
insights.quote: nearly half of firms that have paid tariffs still plan additional price increases to offset these costs, with some expecting to raise prices six months or more in the future

insights.claim: 47 percent of service firms and 44 percent of manufacturers that directly paid tariffs said they have more tariff-induced price increases still to come.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Roughly similar magnitude of pending pass-through across both goods and services suggests broad-based rather than sector-isolated inflationary pressure ahead.
insights.quote: 47 percent of service firms and 44 percent of manufacturers that paid tariffs directly saying they have more tariff-induced price increases to come
insights.locator: More Price Increases Are Coming from Businesses That Paid Tariffs

insights.claim: About 30 percent of tariff-paying service firms plan additional price increases within the next six months, as do nearly 40 percent of tariff-paying manufacturers.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Gives a near-term (sub-6-month) magnitude for how much additional tariff-driven pricing pressure should show up in surveys/CPI over roughly the second half of 2026.
insights.locator: More Price Increases Are Coming from Businesses That Paid Tariffs

insights.claim: 16 percent of service firms and 7 percent of manufacturers that paid tariffs plan to raise prices more than six months from now, meaning some tariff-related inflation won't surface until well into 2027.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Extends the visible tariff-inflation horizon well beyond the current year, a tail investors and policymakers may underweight when assuming pass-through is nearly finished.
insights.locator: More Price Increases Are Coming from Businesses That Paid Tariffs

insights.claim: Just over half of tariff-paying firms fall into categories (insignificant cost impact, already fully passed through, or no plans to raise further) indicating they have no further price increases planned to recoup tariff costs.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: A slim majority have already finished adjusting, so the remaining pipeline is a large minority, not the whole universe of tariff-paying firms — useful for sizing the residual inflation impulse.
insights.locator: Many Businesses Are Not Finished Raising Prices to Offset Tariffs

themes.theme: Who actually bears the tariff cost

insights.claim: Recent research cited in the piece shows nearly 90 percent of the economic burden of tariffs has fallen on U.S. firms and consumers, not foreign exporters.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Confirms tariffs function largely as a domestic tax passed through the supply chain rather than being absorbed abroad, supporting a case for continued consumer-price impact rather than exporter margin compression.
insights.quote: nearly 90 percent of the economic burden of tariffs has fallen on U.S. firms and consumers
insights.locator: Many Businesses Are Not Finished Raising Prices to Offset Tariffs

insights.claim: Two-thirds of service firms and almost all manufacturers surveyed import at least some of their inputs, but only 40 percent of service firms versus 70 percent of manufacturers said they directly paid tariffs in the past twelve months.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Manufacturers are far more directly exposed to tariff costs than services, implying goods-producing firms and their downstream customers carry more direct tariff-inflation risk.
insights.locator: Many Businesses Are Not Finished Raising Prices to Offset Tariffs

insights.claim: Many firms that did not directly pay tariffs still faced higher costs because their suppliers paid the tariffs and passed higher prices upstream, meaning tariff costs cascade through supply chains beyond the direct importer.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Survey figures on direct tariff payers likely understate the true breadth of tariff-cost exposure across the economy, since indirect exposure via suppliers is not fully captured.
insights.locator: Many Businesses Are Not Finished Raising Prices to Offset Tariffs

themes.theme: Why firms are delaying full pass-through

insights.claim: Some businesses operate under fixed-price contracts that prevent them from raising prices until the contracts expire, forcing them to absorb tariff costs temporarily and delaying pass-through.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Contract renewal cycles create a mechanical, somewhat predictable lag structure for when tariff costs finally hit prices — a factor that could be modeled against known contract durations by sector.
insights.quote: long-term contracts can impede businesses from passing through cost increases
insights.locator: Why Businesses Are Planning Future Price Increases

insights.claim: Some firms are deliberately using a 'trickle up' pricing strategy, gradually raising prices over time instead of immediately raising them to fully cover tariffs, to avoid shocking customers while retaining the option to accelerate increases if costs keep rising.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: This is a behavioral pricing framework investors can use to expect drawn-out, incremental margin recovery at firms rather than sharp one-off repricing events — watch for gradual gross-margin improvement over multiple quarters rather than a single step-change.
insights.quote: a “trickle up” approach to price increases, where they gradually raise prices over time rather than immediately raising prices to fully cover tariffs
insights.locator: Why Businesses Are Planning Future Price Increases

insights.claim: Uncertainty about future tariff policy — including potential rate changes, exemptions, or retaliatory tariffs from other countries — is causing some firms to adopt cautious, incremental pricing rather than large, discrete price adjustments.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Ties the pace of price pass-through directly to policy volatility, implying that further tariff rate changes or escalation could extend the inflation-pass-through window even more, an angle for tracking policy-uncertainty indices against pricing surveys.
insights.locator: Why Businesses Are Planning Future Price Increases

insights.claim: The authors argue that even though economists and policymakers often expect tariff-driven price increases to be a one-time price-level adjustment, in practice this adjustment can be a drawn-out affair, especially when tariffs change frequently.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Directly challenges the standard Fed/market framing of tariffs as a transitory, one-off price shock — a contrarian input for anyone modeling how long the Fed should look through tariff-driven inflation before it becomes embedded.
insights.quote: what “one-time” means in practice may be a drawn-out affair, especially when the tariffs change frequently

insights.claim: Because many firms are spreading tariff-related price increases across extended periods, inflationary pressures due to tariffs may persist for some time to come rather than fading quickly.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: A direct forward-looking call that tariff-related inflation persistence should be priced into rate-path and inflation-expectations views over the coming quarters, not treated as already resolved.
insights.quote: inflationary pressures due to tariffs may well last for some time to come

insights.claim: The pattern of gradual tariff pass-through observed here is consistent with a growing body of research showing tariffs pass through to consumer prices incrementally, building over the better part of a year rather than all at once.
insights.speaker: Jaison R. Abel, Mary Amiti, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise, and Nick Montalbano
insights.so_what: Establishes a roughly year-long lag framework for tariff pass-through into consumer prices that can anchor expectations for when the current tariff round's inflation effects should fully materialize and fade.
insights.quote: tariffs pass through to consumer prices incrementally, building over the better part of a year rather than all at once
insights.locator: Many Businesses Are Not Finished Raising Prices to Offset Tariffs
